data.title: How gold loans really work and what lenders don’t highlight
data.description:
Gold loans are often pitched as one of the fastest and easiest ways to borrow money. Walk into a branch with your jewellery, and you can walk out with cash in a matter of hours. No long credit checks, minimal paperwork, and interest rates that look competitive at first glance.

But once you get past the surface, there are a few details that don’t always get enough attention.

How a gold loan actually works

A gold loan is basically a secured loan where you pledge your gold jewellery, and in return, the lender gives you a loan based on its value.

The amount you get depends on the loan-to-value ratio, which is capped by the Reserve Bank of India. Currently, lenders can typically offer up to 75 percent of the gold’s value.

The lender stores the gold safely for the duration of the loan and once you repay the loan along with interest, you get your jewellery back.

The time for which the loan typically lasts is usually just a few months to a year. Some lenders will allow you to renew your loan if you need, but there’s usually an extra cost involved there.

Why people turn to gold loans

Most people find gold loans quick and simple. Since you’re pledging your own gold jewellery, the lender sees it as low risk. That’s why your loan is approved fast.

Your credit score also doesn’t carry as much weight in this situation as it would for something like a personal loan. As, in the end, if you’re unable to pay off the loan, the lender already has your gold.

Interest rates also tend to be lower than credit cards or unsecured loans, which makes gold loans look quite appealing at first.

If you need money urgently, or don’t easily qualify for other loans, this can feel like an easy solution.

The part that often gets missed

Most people don’t know that the interest rate you see in ads for gold loans is not always what you will end up paying. Some lenders quote a lower rate but add processing fees, valuation charges or other costs that push up the overall cost of borrowing.

Interest rates are also charged differently. In some cases, it builds up every month but isn’t paid until the end. If you’re not keeping track, the final amount can come as a surprise.

Repayment structure can vary more than you think

Gold loans don’t always follow the usual EMI pattern people are used to. While some lenders ask you to pay regular monthly EMIs, others are okay if you only pay the interest during the loan tenure, and then settle the full principal at the end.

Some lenders also offer options where you pay everything in one go.

While this flexibility can be useful, some people find it difficult to manage if they’re not organised, and find themselves faced with a big payment at the end of the tenure.

What happens if you miss payments

Since you have pledged your gold, if you are unable to pay your loan on time, your jewellery can be auctioned off to recover the loan.

Lenders will not do this if you miss just one payment, or even a couple, but repeated missed payments can lead to notices and eventually your jewellery will get sold.

If the lender is able to recover the cost of the loan through an auction, all well and good and the lender will return the extra to you.

But it may not be much of a consolation to you as you end up losing your gold. For most people, gold is tied to family, memories, weddings, and years of saving. Losing it can feel far more personal than just a financial setback.

How gold prices affect your loan

Another thing people don’t always think about is how gold prices can affect things while your loan is still ongoing.

If prices drop, the value of the gold you’ve pledged also falls. In some cases, the lender may ask you to reduce your loan amount or add more gold to keep the required margin in place.

It doesn’t happen very often, especially with shorter loans, but it’s still something worth being aware of.

Is it always a good idea

Gold loans can be quite useful, but only in the right situation. They work best when you need money for a short period and are fairly sure you’ll be able to repay it without too much stress. The process is quick, and compared to other options, the cost can be reasonable.

Where things can go wrong is when you start relying on them for longer-term needs or when repayment isn’t certain. The risk of losing your gold is very real, and it’s not something most people are prepared for until it actually happens.

The bottom line

Gold loans seem simple, and in many ways they are, but the details matter more than people realise.

Before taking one, it’s worth understanding the actual cost, how the repayment works, and what could happen if things don’t go as planned.

Used thoughtfully, they can be helpful. Used casually, they can end up being far more expensive, both financially and emotionally.

data.title: U.S. March Payroll Gains Fall Outside 10-Yr Historical WSJ Forecasting Range
data.description:
By Patrick Sullivan

Friday's nonfarm payrolls report found the U.S. gained 178,000 jobs in March, landing outside of a range of Wall Street Journal historical forecasts for the indicator. The unemployment rate of 4.3% was inside a range of Journal historical forecasts for the indicator.

For select economic indicators, including the monthly employment report, Dow Jones Newswires has started measuring whether a reported statistic falls outside a historical range of proprietary Journal forecasting, and if so, by how much. Those forecast ranges are based on an archive of surveys of economists the Journal has conducted over the past decade.

The gauge provides a way to measure the relative volatility of any one particular indicator, compared not just with a contemporary forecast in the days ahead of an indicator's release, but also with its track record of surprising forecasters over time.

Data from the jobs report are being closely watched as investors and economists try to determine any future interest-rate decisions from the Federal Reserve and their assessment of a cooling labor market.

In the case of the March payrolls report, the range of Journal forecasts relative to the actual reported number over that 10-year period was between -38,500 and +73,000. Friday's number came in at 178,000, above the expectation of 59,000 jobs added.

The slight decrease in the unemployment rate fell inside the historical range of -0.2 percentage point to +0.1 percentage point for that data point. The unemployment rate of 4.3%, was below the Wall Street Journal forecasts of 4.4%.

The ranges are made up of the median of historical forecasts that came in lower than the reported number and the median of forecasts that came in higher.

Write to Patrick Sullivan at patrick.sullivan@wsj.com

data.title: Treasury Yields, Dollar Higher Following Robust March Hiring — Market Talk
data.description:
0856 ET - Treasury yields rise and the dollar strengthens following a much stronger-than-expected March jobs report. Payrolls grew by 178,000 versus the 59,000 economists polled by WSJ expected. The unemployment rate edged down to 4.3% from 4.4%. Health care added 76,000 jobs last month. Employment in ambulatory health care services rose by 54,000, reflecting an increase of 35,000 in offices of physicians as workers returned from a strike. The 10-year Treasury yield is 4.35% versus 4.31% Thursday, and the 2-year Treasury yield is 3.85% versus 3.79%. The dollar strengthens against the euro and the yen. (patrick.sheridan@wsj.com)

0427 ET - The U.S.'s 100% pharmaceutical tariffs are likely to have limited effect on the sector, says ING's Diederik Stadig in a note. The economist expects the tariff to apply to branded pharmaceuticals for which no exemptions have been negotiated, which would mainly affect economies such as Singapore, India and China, he says. However, these countries don't export substantial volumes of such products to the U.S. The tariff should be seen as a warning for more intense competition between the U.S. and China's biotech and pharma sectors, he says, noting that China has developed into a biopharma innovation hub. ING estimates that around one-third of all new molecules that are in global pipelines originate in China, compared with 4% 12 years ago. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0357 ET - Chinese exporters may again inadvertently benefit from the global shock from the Middle East conflict, Nomura analysts write in a note. Over the past six years, China's export sector has demonstrated remarkable resilience during periods when overseas economies encountered severe supply chain disruptions, including the Covid-19 and the outbreak of Russia-Ukraine was, they say. While the current crisis is hurting China's energy imports, China's manufacturers and exporters have a stable supply of electricity, largely insulated from the current crisis, they add. They note China's power supply uses almost no natural gas and oil while relying heavily on coal which is mainly mined in China, they say.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0200 ET - The BOJ could raise rates this month, Barclays economists say in a note. They cite factors including the hawkish tone of the Summary of Opinions from the BOJ's March meeting and a weaker yen. However, two weeks have passed since the March meeting and risks of a prolonged Middle East conflict seem to have increased. It's possible that more BOJ board members will shift toward a view that an economic slump in Japan will become more serious. Hence, the decision on whether to raise rates at the April 27-28 meeting could remain uncertain until the "very end," the economists add. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0117 ET - Singapore's inflation is likely to be contained in 2026 despite rising from the year before, says Fitch Ratings in a report. The city-state's robust wage growth and imported price pressure, particularly for energy and food, are likely to drive headline inflation higher to 2.0% in 2026 from 0.9% in 2025, Fitch says. Still, Singapore has strong external buffers against the ongoing oil shock, Fitch says. Its exchange-rate-based monetary policy is also likely to limit imported inflation, the ratings firm adds. Singapore's central bank expects core and headline inflation to come in at 1.0%-2.0% this year.(megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0051 ET - Singapore's near-term economic growth is likely to moderate, says Fitch Ratings in a report. The city-state's artificial intelligence-driven upswing in exports and investment are likely to tail off in 2027, Fitch says. While Singapore's multi-year construction projects and robust activity in externally-oriented sectors should keep growth above potential, Fitch flags the island nation's exposure to global supply chain disruptions and shifts in global trade policy and demand. The ratings firm projects Singapore's economic growth to ease to 3.5% in 2026 and 3.0% in 2027, after a 5.0% gain in 2025, but notes these forecasts face downside risks. It foresees the city-state's medium-term growth at around 2.5%.(megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0011 ET - Higher energy prices will likely have indirect inflation effects on non-fuel items globally, says Simon MacAdam, economist at Capital Economics. Higher energy prices will prompt firms to pass on higher fuel costs to consumers, boosting the prices of non-fuel items, such as electricity, food and transportation, the economist notes. Food production is energy-intensive, especially in relation to fertilizers, he says, adding that food inflation then increases price pressures in the hospitality sectors. An energy price shock can also increase the costs of production of energy-intensive materials like refined metals, processed rubber and wood pulp, he adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2353 ET - China's manufacturing activity is likely to remain resilient in the near term, Barclays economists say in a research note. The country's official manufacturing purchasing managers' index rose to 50.4 in March from 49.0 in February, while production and new orders also returned to expansion. "The PMIs suggest limited impact from the ongoing Iran conflict on China's supply and demand," Barclays says. The new export orders subindex hit a two-year high on green energy-related demand, underscoring how a faster global green transition is benefiting China, the bank says. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

2259 ET - The temporary energy supply disruption amid the Iran conflict could weigh on Malaysia, prompting Hong Leong IB to lower its 2026 GDP growth forecast to 4.5% from 4.7%. Although Malaysian vessels were recently granted free passage through the Strait of Hormuz, crude oil supply risks cannot be fully discounted, analysts say in note. Growth is projected to be supported by strong electronic exports and continued consumption, they say. Upward price pressures on RON97 fuel, unsubsidized diesel, electricity and food are expected amid higher commodity costs and unfavorable weather, prompting a 2026 CPI growth forecast revision to 2.0% from 1.7% previously. With inflation risks tilted upward and growth prospects moderating, Bank Negara may maintain its policy rate at 2.75% while assessing the conflict's impact, they add. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2257 ET - Taiwan's consumer prices likely rose 1.7% on year in March, according to a WSJ poll of five economists. The print may reflect early signs of price pressures from the energy shock as the Middle East conflict continues. While most economists expect Taiwan's March print to remain under the 2% target watched by the central bank, Deutsche Bank forecasts it to rise above 2% for the first time in nearly a year to 2.2%. With inflation risks rising and economic growth robust, economists reckon that the island's monetary policy could shift to a hike rather than a cut later this year. Still, unless inflation significantly overshoots expectations in the coming months, policymakers will likely stay on hold when they next meet in June, ING economists say. Inflation data is due next Wednesday. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2252 ET - Japan's service sector activity stayed solid in March, but there are tentative signs that growth momentum was beginning to fade in March, S&P Global PMI data show. Both output and new order indexes retreated from the recent highs seen in February, coinciding with the outbreak of the war in the Middle East, which contributed to intensifying cost pressures, says Annabel Fiddes at S&P Global Market Intelligence. Firms recorded the steepest increase in expenses since last April, driving another historically strong rise in selling prices, the economist says. Uncertainty over how long the war will last, and how it will affect global demand and inflation, dimmed business confidence in March, she adds. Overall optimism toward the outlook for business activity slipped to the lowest since the pandemic. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

2245 ET - The U.S. dollar could stay around 4.05 Malaysian ringgit next week, Kenanga economists say in a note. U.S. non-farm payrolls data due later will be closely watched for signs of slowing labor-market momentum, they say. While higher energy costs have raised speculation for the Fed's potential rate increase, Chair Jerome Powell said medium-term inflation expectations remain well anchored, suggesting there is no entrenched tightening bias, they say. Assuming Middle East tensions ease by 3Q ahead of the U.S. mid-term election, improved energy flows could support the ringgit, with markets likely to resume pricing in the Fed's possible rate cuts in 4Q, they add. The dollar is down 0.1% at 4.0345 ringgit.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: TaxDome Publishes First-of-Its-Kind "Accounting Industry Index" Revealing Client Bases Grew 22% in 2025
data.description:
Based on operational data from 15,000+ firms, the Index shows how top practices are increasing revenue, streamlining operations and reducing workload.

NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 3, 2026 / Accounting firms across the United States are growing and becoming more efficient and profitable, according to operational data from more than 15,000 firms in the Accounting Industry Index by TaxDome. The result is the largest dataset of its kind ever published in the accounting profession.

Most industry reports rely on how firms describe their own performance. The Accounting Industry Index is the first benchmarking report for the accounting profession built entirely on real operational data, so firms can benchmark their performance against top firms of the same size. Every finding in the Index is derived from anonymized data generated by firms actively using TaxDome - covering client interactions, document workflows, billing patterns, and payment behavior from 2025.

"When we looked at how accounting firms measure growth, we saw that many are flying blind. They feel like they're growing, but don't know how their growth compares - or whether it's efficient and sustainable. So we analyzed firms operating on a single system and uncovered clear, repeatable growth patterns. This Index shares those benchmarks, replacing guesswork with clarity." - Petar Iliev, Chief Revenue Officer, TaxDome

While client growth remains a headline metric, the data shows a more important trend: firms are simultaneously increasing revenue and efficiency. Team sizes expanded by 10%, while gross merchandise value processed through the platform rose 27%, outpacing both client and team growth. Firms are serving more clients with relatively smaller teams, indicating strong profitability gains across the industry.

The report breaks down performance by firm size, offering cohort-specific benchmarks that allow practices to assess how they compare to peers at the same stage of growth.

Among the report's other key findings: 90% of clients who first log in to the TaxDome portal continue using it, signaling a major shift toward clients embracing centralized client portals. At the same time, client bases grew by an average of 22% YoY in 2025, confirming that demand for accounting services remains strong and broad-based across firm sizes, from solo practitioners to practices with 25+ employees.

This shift is driven by how firms operate, particularly in managing client experience and billing workflows. The report shows that firms using centralized client collaboration through portals and mobile apps achieve higher engagement, while embedding billing into delivery workflows reduces time-to-payment.

TaxDome plans to publish the Accounting Industry Index regularly. Future editions will track how benchmarks evolve as the profession adapts to changing demand, workforce constraints, and technology adoption-helping firms evaluate their performance against leading practices.

About TaxDome

TaxDome is the leading practice management platform for tax, bookkeeping, and accounting firms, serving more than 15,000 full-service accounting practices worldwide. With a top-rated client app and portal plus a customizable firm operation platform, TaxDome unifies client communication, document management, e-signatures, AI-powered automation, proposals, intake & delivery, billing, and payment workflows in a single connected system. TaxDome is headquartered in New York City.

Contact Information:

Kara Prior

Taxdome

+1 833-829-3663

marketing@taxdome.com

SOURCE: Taxdome

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: U.S. Employment Jumps By 178,000 Jobs In March, Much More Than Expected
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - The Labor Department released a report on Friday showing employment in the U.S. jumped by much more than expected in the month of March.

The report said non-farm payroll employment shot up by 178,000 jobs in March after plunging by a revised 133,000 jobs in February.

Economists had expected employment to climb by 51,000 jobs compared to the loss of 92,000 jobs originally reported for the previous month.

The Labor Department also said the unemployment rate edged down to 4.3 percent in March from 4.4 percent in February. The unemployment rate was expected to remain unchanged.

data.title: Hong Zhifang Discloses Investment in DarkIris Inc. with 27.08% Stake
data.description:
Hong Zhifang disclosed a 27.08% stake in DarkIris Inc.’s ordinary shares through a Schedule 13D. The filing, accepted on April 03, 2026, reflects holdings built via Class B shares issued in 2024 and 2025. The position carries super-voting power—20 votes per Class B share—and the shares can convert into Class A on a one-for-one basis.

Investor Intent

Hong Zhifang says he "do not have any present plans or proposals" to change control or pursue major corporate actions, but he "may review, reconsider and change their position" over time and could seek to influence management.

He agreed to a six-month lock-up after DarkIris Inc.’s IPO that limits transfers of the 5,449,600 Class B shares. He may still evaluate future steps, including potential conversions or discussions with the company, after the lock-up ends.

Investor's Background

Hong Zhifang is the chief executive officer, chairman, and a director of DarkIris Inc. He holds his stake through RongStar Holdings Ltd, a British Virgin Islands investment holding company he wholly owns. He focuses on building DarkIris while maintaining control through high-vote shares and uses holding vehicles for long-term ownership.

Original SEC Filing: DarkIris Inc. [ DKI ] - SCHEDULE 13D - Apr. 03, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: AirSculpt Technologies Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• AirSculpt Technologies Inc reported a quarterly adjusted loss of 2 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of -9 cents. The mean expectation of two analysts for the quarter was for a loss of 2 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from -3 cents to -2 cents per share.

  • Revenue fell 14.6% to $33.44 million from a year ago; analysts expected $34.51 million.

  • AirSculpt Technologies Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 2 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $1.28 million.

  • AirSculpt Technologies Inc shares had fallen by 3.5% this quarter and gained 37.9% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 16.7% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no negative revisions of earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 1 "strong buy" or "buy," 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the healthcare facilities & services peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for AirSculpt Technologies Inc is $6.50, about 51.2% above its last closing price of $3.17

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data April 3 at 12:38 p.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: Trump says US can take Strait of Hormuz with more time
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Friday said the U.S. can open the Strait of Hormuz with a little more time, as pressure mounts for his administration to find a quick resolution to a war against Iran.

"With a little more time, we can easily OPEN THE HORMUZ STRAIT, TAKE THE OIL,& MAKE A FORTUNE," Trump said in a post on Truth Social.

Nearly five weeks after it started ⁠with a joint U.S.-Israeli aerial assault, the war in Iran continues to spread chaos across the region and roil financial markets, raising the pressure on Trump to find a quick resolution to the conflict.

Iran has effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz , a key waterway that carries about a fifth of the world's total oil consumption, in retaliation for U.S.-Israeli ​strikes that began in late February. Reopening it has become a priority for governments around the world as energy prices soar.

In the speech on Wednesday ​night, Trump repeated his threats against Iran's civilian power plants and gave no clear timeline for ending hostilities, drawing vows of retaliation from Iran and depressing share prices.

data.title: GMG Verified by Advanced Carbons Council & Successful ISO9001 Audit
data.description:
BRISBANE, AUS, Apr 1, 2026 - (ACN Newswire) - Graphene Manufacturing Group Ltd (TSXV: GMG) (OTCQX: GMGMF) ("GMG" or the "Company") is pleased that the Advanced Carbons Council (the "ACC") has certified GMG as a Verified Graphene Producer.

The Verified Graphene Producer program is an independent 3rd party verification by the ACC of not just the material, but it includes an in-person visit of the production facilities. This makes it unique in that the ACC verify the source of the material as coming directly from the production stream while using world-class characterization labs to test the material.

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/8082/290829_3bb80a87648f4a4f_001full.jpg

The Verified Graphene Producer program follows the Graphene Classification Framework (GCF) and the currently published international standards, including: ISO/TS 9651:2025 Nanotechnologies - Classification framework for graphene-related 2D materials, ISO/TR 19733:2019(E), Nanotechnologies - Matrix of properties and measurement techniques for graphene and related two dimensional (2D) materials, ISO/TS 80004-13:2017(E) Nanotechnologies - Vocabulary - Part 13: Graphene and related two-dimensional (2D) materials and ISO/TS 21356-1:2021(E) Nanotechnologies - Structural characterization of graphene - Part 1: Graphene from powders and dispersions

The Company is also pleased to announce it has successfully passed another ISO9001:2015 quality audit of the organisation for the manufacturing, distributing, and providing technical support for the Company's graphene coating and lubricant additive products. Figure 1 shows the certificate. ISO 9001 is the internationally recognized standard for Quality Management Systems (QMS). It provides a framework for organizations to ensure products and services consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements while focusing on continual improvement. It is applicable to any industry or business size, focusing on quality control, efficiency, and risk management.

Terrance Barkan, Executive Director of the Advanced Carbons Council, commented "We are proud to count Graphene Manufacturing Group (GMG) as the newest member of a select group of graphene companies to have passed a rigorous in-person inspection of their facilities and a thorough examination of their graphene material. Independent, third party validation through the Verified Graphene Producer program is an incredibly valuable tool for customers, investors and other stakeholders when choosing a graphene producer/supplier. "

Figure 1: GMG's ISO9001 Quality Management Standards Certification

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit: https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/8082/290829_3bb80a87648f4a4f_002full.jpg

Craig Nicol, CEO & Managing Director of the Company, commented "Verification by the Advanced Carbon Council as one of five currently verified global graphene producers is another proof of the high quality of our graphene and why our graphene products function at such a world leading standard and our quality system audit successful outcome shows our ability to turn this into a real product manufacturing business."

Jack Perkowski, Chairman and Non-Executive Director of the Company, commented: "Congratulations to the team on getting this third-party verification on the graphene and quality management system - a real table stake when it comes to producing carbon nano material products".

About Advanced Carbons Council: The Advanced Carbons Council (ACC) is the global trade association that supports the production, adoption and use of engineered advanced carbons. Our mission is to connect and facilitate the cross-sector fertilization of ideas, applications and business for all advanced materials producers and users. Our Members are companies and organizations that produce, use and develop applications for: Graphene, Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs), Carbon Nanofibers, Carbon Fiber, Synthetic and Engineered Graphite, Carbon-Carbon Composites, Carbon Nanodiamonds (CNDs), Fullerenes, MXenes and new carbon forms, Recycled and Reclaimed Carbons. Delivered by the same team that has grown and developed The Graphene Council over the past 12 years to become a global community of more than 40,000 materials professionals, the ACC focuses on standards, quality, safety, education, sustainability, advocacy, verification, testing and outreach to connect users with qualified supply chain partners.

About GMG:

GMG is an Australian based clean-technology company which develops, makes and sells energy saving and energy storage solutions, enabled by graphene manufactured via in house production process. GMG uses its own proprietary production process to decompose natural gas (i.e. methane) into its natural elements, carbon (as graphene), hydrogen and some residual hydrocarbon gases. This process produces high quality, low cost, scalable, 'tuneable' and low/no contaminant graphene suitable for use in clean-technology and other applications.

The Company's present focus is to de-risk and develop commercial scale-up capabilities, and secure market applications. In the energy savings segment, GMG has initially focused on graphene enhanced heating, ventilation and air conditioning ("HVAC-R") coating (or energy-saving coating) which is now being marketed into other applications including electronic heat sinks, industrial process plants and data centres. Another product GMG has developed is the graphene lubricant additive focused on saving liquid fuels initially for diesel engines.

In the energy storage segment, GMG and the University of Queensland are working collaboratively with financial support from the Australian Government to progress R&D and commercialization of graphene aluminium-ion batteries ("G+AI Batteries"). GMG has also developed a graphene additive slurry that is aimed at improving the performance of lithium-ion batteries.

GMG's 4 critical business objectives are:

1. Produce Graphene and improve/scale cell production processes

2. Build Revenue from Energy Savings Products

3. Develop Next-Generation Battery

4. Develop Supply Chain, Partners & Project Execution Capability

For further information please contact:

Craig Nicol, Chief Executive Officer & Managing Director of the Company at craig.nicol@graphenemg.com, +61 415 445 223

Leo Karabelas at Focus Communications Investor Relations, leo@fcir.ca, +1 647 689 6041

data.title: iQSTEL Inc expected to post a loss of 23 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• iQSTEL Inc is expected to report resultson April 7 for the period ending December 31 2025

  • The Coral Gables Florida-based company is expected to report revenue of $104.55 million, according to the estimate from one analyst, based on LSEG data.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for iQSTEL Inc is for a loss of 23 cents per share.

  • The one available analyst rating on the shares is "buy".

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for iQSTEL Inc is $17.69, about 953.2% above its last closing price of $1.68

data.title: Dell Stock Jumps -- It Rides AI Wave, Servers Soar 350%
data.description:
Dell Technologies shares climbed about 3% on Thursday as investors assessed the company's strong AI-driven growth and a low valuation.

The stock has risen roughly 35% year to date and about 85% over the past 12 months. It trades at 12 times forward earnings, a multiple some analysts view as modest relative to its growth trajectory.

In the fiscal fourth quarter ended Jan. 30, Dell's infrastructure solutions group, which includes AI servers and networking equipment, posted revenue of $19.6 billion, up 73% from a year earlier. AI-optimized server revenue jumped more than 350%, while networking revenue rose 27%.

The company's client solutions group, primarily its PC business, saw revenue increase 16% to $11.6 billion. Dell expects AI-optimized server revenue to grow about 103% in the current fiscal year, with total revenue projected to rise 23% and earnings per share up 33%.

The company has positioned itself as an AI factory in partnership with Nvidia , providing hardware and systems for data centers and cloud providers. Dell's operating income in the infrastructure segment rose 41% to $2.9 billion during the quarter.
